concerns.text: The plan proposes comparing model activation time-series (discrete token steps) directly to human MEG data (continuous time, ~1000Hz sampling) via Phase Locking Value (PLV). The 'Mapping Hypothesis' in spec.md (1 token = 10ms) is arbitrary and lacks empirical grounding. Without a validated temporal scaling factor, the PLV calculation will measure alignment to an arbitrary phase shift rather than biological synchrony, invalidating the construct validity of the 'Neural Alignment' metric.

The plan assumes a single forward pass of DistilBERT (static, non-recurrent) generates a 'time-series' suitable for spectral analysis (FFT/PLV). Transformers process sequences in parallel; there is no intrinsic temporal evolution of activation within a single forward pass to analyze for 40Hz oscillations unless the model is unrolled over time or the 'time' axis is artificially constructed from token positions. The plan does not specify how the 'ActivationTimeSeries' is generated from a static forward pass, risking a category error where token position is mistaken for neural time.

The plan relies on OpenNeuro ds000246 for 'binding task' signatures. However, the spec acknowledges the dataset may lack a clean 'feature binding' condition, falling back to 'general task-evoked gamma'. Comparing a specific computational intervention (oscillatory attention) to a general, non-specific gamma response (which occurs in many cognitive tasks) introduces a massive confound. The control condition (non-oscillatory model) must be compared against the *same* specific condition to isolate the binding effect; using a general proxy invalidates the causal inference of the intervention.

The plan proposes a frequency sweep (20-60Hz) to test the 40Hz hypothesis. However, the 'token processing rate' is fixed by the model architecture and input sequence length. Injecting a 40Hz sinusoid into a system processing 50 tokens in a single forward pass (static) creates a frequency that is mathematically decoupled from the biological 40Hz unless the 'time' axis is explicitly defined. If the 'time' axis is token index, 40Hz implies 40 cycles per token, which is physically impossible to resolve with a single scalar per token. The methodology fails to define the sampling rate (Fs) required to make the frequency sweep meaningful.
